question_title: Will the STF proclaim the final result/thesis of the big-tech 'embargos de declaração' in the Marco Civil case before June 28, 2026?
summary: The STF has explicitly confirmed June 17, 2026 as the date for proclaiming the final result and thesis of the Marco Civil embargos de declaração, per Conjur, Metrópoles, and STF's own news portal. Voting was substantially completed on June 10-11 with a majority formed on the 60-day adaptation period and other key elements — what remains is primarily procedural text harmonization and analysis of 3 remaining embargos. Even if June 17 proves insufficient, there are at least 3-4 additional plenary sessions (June 18, 24, 25) before the June 27 deadline. The main downside risks are STF's documented tendency toward procedural delays (this proclamation was already delayed once), remaining ministerial divergences on scope and retroactivity, and the possibility (though unlikely at this stage) of a pedido de vista. The 92% probability reflects high confidence that the proclamation will occur within the generous 2-week window, tempered by genuine institutional uncertainty about Brazilian Supreme Court timing.

factors.description: June 17 explicitly confirmed by STF and multiple credible sources (Conjur, Metrópoles, STF news) as date for proclamation of result and thesis
Voting substantially completed June 10-11 with majority formed on key issues - remaining work is primarily procedural text harmonization
Multiple backup plenary sessions available (June 18, 24, 25) if June 17 doesn't complete the process - provides significant safety margin within the June 27 deadline
STF has documented history of last-minute postponements, and this very case's proclamation was already delayed once (from June 11 to a later date)
Remaining substantive divergences among ministers on user threshold, retroactivity, and solidary liability could complicate thesis text harmonization
3 of 12 embargos de declaração still need to be analyzed, adding complexity to the remaining sessions

reasoning:
**Forecast: 92% probability (YES)**

**Key reasoning:**
- **June 17 is explicitly confirmed** by the STF and multiple credible Brazilian media outlets as the date for the formal proclamation of the result and thesis
- **Voting is essentially complete** — the STF concluded analysis of 9/12 embargos on June 10-11, forming a majority on key elements including the 60-day platform adaptation period
- **Multiple backup sessions exist** (June 18, 24, 25) before the June 27 deadline, providing a substantial safety margin
- **Risks discounted**: STF's documented history of delays (-), remaining ministerial divergences on scope/retroactivity (-), and 3 remaining embargos still to be analyzed (-)

The ~8% NO probability accounts for the genuine possibility of institutional delays, a pedido de vista, or unexpected complications that push the proclamation past all available sessions before June 27.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the STF proclaim the final result/thesis of the big-tech 'embargos de declaração' in the Marco Civil case before June 28, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44057)
[Maioria do STF quer que plataformas sigam Marco Civil em 60 dias](https://www.conjur.com.br/2026-jun-11/stf-tem-maioria-pelo-prazo-de-60-dias-para-plataformas-se-adequarem-a-tese-do-marco-civil/)

[STF dá 60 dias para big techs aplicarem alterações no Marco Civil, mas deixa tese pendente](https://www.metropoles.com/colunas/manoela-alcantara/stf-da-60-dias-para-big-techs-aplicarem-alteracoes-no-marco-civil-mas-deixa-tese-pendente)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves YES if, between June 13, 2026 and June 27, 2026 (inclusive), the STF plenary formally proclaims the result of the big-tech "embargos de declaração" in the Marco Civil da Internet case (RE 1.037.396 / RE 1.057.258, Themes 987 and 533), thereby fixing the final wording of the thesis ("tese"). It resolves NO if no such proclamation has occurred by the end of June 27, 2026 (Brasília time, UTC-3).

Resolution will be based on official STF communications via the STF portal (https://portal.stf.jus.br/) — for example a news item reporting that the plenary "proclamou o resultado" / concluded ("encerrou"/"concluiu") the judgment of these embargos and fixed the thesis — or on reporting by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) such as Agência Brasil, O Globo, Folha de S.Paulo or Conjur. A clear report that the plenary has proclaimed/closed the result and set the final thesis text suffices for YES.
fine_print: "Proclamation of the result" means the plenary's official announcement closing the judgment and fixing the final thesis text, as reported by the STF or credible sources. The earlier completion of voting on 10-11 June 2026 does NOT by itself resolve the question YES, because the formal proclamation of the result and final thesis was expressly postponed; only an event dated June 13-27, 2026 (Brasília time) that the STF or credible sources describe as the proclamation/closing of the result and fixing of the thesis counts. A mere publication of the judgment minutes (ata) or of the full written acórdão, without a plenary proclamation of the result, also counts as YES if the STF or credible sources report that the result/thesis has been formally fixed. If the proclamation is postponed beyond June 27, 2026, or the question cannot be determined from the listed sources by that date, it resolves NO.

factors.description: June 17 explicitly confirmed by STF and multiple credible sources as date for proclamation of result and thesis
Voting substantially completed June 10-11 with majority formed - remaining work is primarily procedural text harmonization
Multiple backup plenary sessions available (June 18, 24, 25) if June 17 doesn't complete the process
STF history of last-minute postponements and this case has already been delayed once
Remaining divergences among ministers on user threshold, retroactivity, and solidary liability could complicate thesis harmonization
3 remaining embargos still need to be analyzed, adding complexity

input.anchor: STF court proceedings that have been scheduled for a specific date with voting already completed resolve on time approximately 80-85% of the time. Base rate for procedural completion within a multi-week window with multiple backup dates is higher.
input.assessment: The evidence strongly favors YES. The STF has explicitly scheduled the proclamation for June 17, 2026, and this is confirmed by the court itself and multiple credible Brazilian media outlets. The substantive work is done - voting concluded June 10-11 with a majority formed. What remains is text harmonization and formal proclamation, which is largely procedural. Even accounting for STF's documented history of delays (including one prior delay in this very case), there are 5 plenary sessions available before the June 27 deadline. The probability of the proclamation being delayed past ALL of these sessions (June 17, 18, 24, 25) is quite low. The main risk is a 'pedido de vista' or some unexpected political complication, but these are unlikely given the advanced state of voting and the momentum to conclude. A probability of ~92% seems appropriate - high but not near-certain, reflecting the genuine risk of institutional delays.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor of ~85% for scheduled court proceedings with completed voting. Upgraded by the multi-session safety margin (5 sessions in the window). Downgraded slightly by STF's postponement history. Final estimate of 92% reflects strong but not ironclad evidence for YES.
input.key_uncertainties: Main uncertainty is whether unexpected political dynamics, a pedido de vista, or a dispute over thesis wording could delay the proclamation beyond ALL available sessions through June 25. Secondary uncertainty is whether the 3 remaining embargos could introduce complications.
input.tool_audit: Research tool provided comprehensive information from STF official news, Conjur, CNN Brasil, and other credible sources. Web search confirmed June 17 date from multiple independent sources. URL fetch from Conjur and Metrópoles provided detailed confirmation. All tools performed well.
input.update_triggers: 1. News that the June 17 session has been cancelled or the Marco Civil case removed from the agenda would significantly reduce probability. 2. A minister requesting a pedido de vista would be a major negative signal. 3. News of successful proclamation on June 17 would resolve the question YES.
input.process_reflection: This was a straightforward question with good data availability. The research tool efficiently gathered relevant Brazilian legal news. The main challenge was the language barrier (Portuguese), but the tools handled it well. The question is well-structured with clear resolution criteria.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Hallucinated evidence: None found.** All six factors are well-grounded in the research trace:

- **Factor 1 (June 17 confirmed):** Conjur snippet explicitly states "A tese e o resultado final serão proclamados na próxima quarta-feira (17/6)." Metrópoles confirms: "será proclamada na próxima quarta-feira (17/6)." STF news: "Julgamento deverá ser retomado na próxima quarta (17)." Multiple independent sources confirm.
- **Factor 2 (Voting substantially completed):** Migalhas: "Corte concluiu análise dos recursos, mas adiou a proclamação do resultado." Research confirms 9 of 12 embargos addressed with majority formed on 60-day adaptation period.
- **Factor 3 (Backup sessions):** STF plenary calendar research identifies June 18, 24, and 25 as expected additional sessions, though only June 17 was explicitly confirmed in sources.
- **Factor 4 (History of postponements):** Grounded in the question background itself and the fact that this case was already delayed once (from June 11).
- **Factor 5 (Remaining divergences):** Research details specific divergences: user threshold (Toffoli vs Dino), retroactivity (4 ministers opposing), solidary liability (Mendonça/Nunes Marques).
- **Factor 6 (3 remaining embargos):** Research confirms "Three additional embargos also remain to be analyzed."

**Double-counting: Minor, acknowledged by agent.** Factors 1 and 2 draw on partially overlapping evidence (the Conjur article supports both the scheduling claim and the voting-completion claim). However, they capture genuinely distinct informational content (scheduling certainty vs. substantive completion). The agent explicitly noted this overlap in the trace and discounted the raw factor-implied probability (~96.6%) down to 92%.

**Wrong-direction factors: None.** Positive factors (1, 2, 3) correctly favor YES; negative factors (4, 5, 6) correctly favor NO.

**Resolution misalignment: None.** The agent correctly engaged with the resolution criteria's specific terms — that the June 10-11 voting does NOT resolve YES, that only a proclamation dated June 13-27 counts, and that the question is about the formal proclamation as reported by STF or credible sources.

**Pre-publication event: Properly handled.** The agent did not treat the June 10-11 voting as already resolving the question.

**Probability assessment: Reasonable.** At 92%, the estimate reflects: (a) a confirmed date from the court and multiple credible sources, (b) substantive work already complete, (c) 4+ backup sessions through June 25, while appropriately discounting for (d) STF's documented history of delays, (e) one prior delay in this very case, and (f) remaining divergences among ministers. My independent estimate would be ~90-94%, consistent with the agent's 92%.

**Anchor divergence: Justified.** The 80-85% anchor is departed by ~7-12pp, driven primarily by Factor 1 (logit +2.5, June 17 explicitly confirmed by multiple sources). This is a strong, verified factor that justifies the departure.

**Counterargument: Adequate.** The counterargument identifies pedido de vista, packed late-June docket, and substantive (not cosmetic) ministerial divergences as real NO scenarios, with specific probability adjustments for each.

**Confidence in estimate (0.72): Appropriate.** Not overconfident given the niche topic and genuine uncertainty about STF procedural dynamics.

**Tool audit: Trivially inaccurate.** Agent claims "All tools performed well" but there was 1 Wikipedia error (4.3% rate). This error was irrelevant to the core research, so no impact on the forecast.

**Worldview consistency: No conflicts found.** The worldview research entries for this topic are consistent with the factors and assessment.

No errors found that would change the forecast direction or magnitude.
